content.full_text:
Re
Fnrn Polrucel PnacrrcEs CoupussroN
428 J Street e Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
November 14,2005
Lori J. Barker
Assistant City Attomey
Office of the City Attorney
P.O. Box 3420
Chico, CA 95927
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-05-203
Dear Ms. Barker:
This letter is in response on behalf of Councilmember Larry Wahl for advice
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act").1
QUESTION
Is Councilmember Wahl presumed to have a disqualiffing conflict of interest in
decisions regarding the Enloe Project?
CONCLUSION
Yes. It is presumed that the financial effect of the governmental decision on
Councilmember Wahl's real property interest is material. Therefore, unless this
presumption is rebutted or an exception applies, he is disqualified from participating in
decisions regarding the Enloe Project if it is reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental
decision will have any frnancial effect - even a penny's worth - on his real property.
FACTS
Councilmember Wahl's spouse has an ownership
three parcels of property located at the intersection of 8m
(8th Avenue Property).
interest worth over $2,000 in
Avenue and Magnolia Avenue
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
a€,e,


File No. A-05-203
Page No. 2
Enloe Medical Center (Enloe) owns Enloe Hospital and anumber of other
facilities and properties located in the City of Chico which comprise the Enloe medical
campus.
Enloe has adopted a master plan which calls for the construction of a number of
improvements within the Enloe medical campus, including an expansion of the existing
hospital, two parking structures, the closure of a portion of a public street and the
installation of various traffic calming devices.
Implementation of Enloe's master plan requires a number of city approvals and
Enloe has submitted a number of applications to the city, including applicat^ions for the
tezone of several properties, amendments to the general plan land use designations for
several properties, a development agreement, a planned development p".-it and a street
abandonment.
The boundary of Enloe medical czunpus, as depicted on the applications submitted
by Enloe, is located within 500 feet of the 8fr Avenue property.
Pursuant to the submitted applications, all of the properties proposed to be
rezorred or have their general plan land use designation amended are located over 500
feet from the 8ft Avenue rroperty. The proposed street abandonment and all
improveme.nts to be constructed are proposed to be on properties located over 500 feet
from the 8ft Avenue Property, with tie exception of the installation of sidewalk bulbing
and stop signs at the intersection of 7ft and Magnolia.
It is not yet known what properties will be subject to the proposed development
agreement as only avery preliminary draft of that agreement has been presented.
Because all of the applications relate to an integrated project, it is anticipated that
they will be discussed by the council as one item.
After the applications were received by the city, adraft EIR was prepared and
circulated and public comments were received. In response to some of those comments,
Enloe has submitted a letter to the city proposing some modifications to its project. One
of the modifications is to build one parking structure, instead of two, and to locate it on
the corner of 7th and Magnolia. Under this-propos al, the structure would be located on a
parcel that is within 500 feet of the 8ft Avenue Property. It is anticipated that this
modification will be considered at the time that the Enloe applications come before the
city council.


File No. A-05-203
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
I & 2. Is Councilmember Wahl a 66public official" and will he be making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act pertain only to public officials. A
public official includes ooevery member, officer, empioyre or consuliant of a state or local
govemmentagency . . . ." (section 82D48;regulation 1g701(a)(1).) As an elected
member of the city council, Councilmember Wahl is a public official subject to the Act,s
conflict of interest provisions.
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contr-actual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 19702.1.)
Councilmember Wahl will "make a goveflrmental decision" if he votes on any
issue obligating the city with respect to the Enloe Project. In addition to actually voting
on these matters, if he engages in negotiations without significant substantive review, -
provides advice or makes recommendations, he will be "particip ating" in a decision
(Regulation 18702.2). He will also be "influencing" thaidecision if she appears before or
otherwise atiempt to influence any member, officei employee or consultant of the
agency." (Regulation 1 87 02.3.)
3. What are Councilmember Wahl's economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest, in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial "t",, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any oith" official,s economic
interests, described as follows:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87r03(a); Regulation 1g703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 1 03 (d); Regulation 1 8703. 1 (b));
a
2 If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including city council members) and he or
she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, thenhe or she must: (lj immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identiS, each type of econJmic interest involved in the decision as
well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in rigulation l87O2.5,subdivision (b)(l)(B), on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration oithe
discussion atdlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and qpeaking as a
member of the public regarding personal interests, special ru1es found in regulation ll7o2:s,subdivisions
(c) and (d) apply. ($ 87105). Since a city council member is a position enumerated in Section 87200, these
requirements apply to Councilmember Wahl.


a
File No. A-05-203
Page No. 4
An economic interest in real property inwhich he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section s7103(b);regulation 1s703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to thetecision (Section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section g7103(e); reguiation
18703.4).
a
a
In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the ,,personal
financial effects" rule (Section 87103; regulation 19703.5).
Real Property Interest:
"Interest in real property" includes:
"...any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to
acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction
owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or
other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market ,uir"
of the interest is two thousand dollars ($ 2,000) or more. Interests
in real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of
interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the
individual or immediatefamily owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 
-szozz.
Emphasis added.)
Since Councilmernber Wahl's spouse has an ownership interest of more than
$2,000 in the Sft Avenue Property, and she is a member of his "immediate f*ifv,#ifr"
council member has an economic interest in that property under section 82033.
4. fs Councilmember Wahl's economic interest directly or indirecfly
involved in the governmental decision?
You have stated that the decision is regarding the implementation of Enloe,s
master plan "relate to an integrated project" and "it is anticipated that they will be
discussed by the (city) council as one item." As such, the Enloe project is the subject of
3 "Immediate family" includes the spouse and dependents of an official. (Section g2029.) Since
lhe 9_wner of this real property is the council member's ,po,rr", and thus a member of his ,.immediate
family," the council member has an economic interest in-that property under section g2033.


File No. A-05-203
Page No. 5
the decision, and the property of the council member's spouse will be directly involved in
the decision since it is located within 500 feet of the proposed parking structures and the
installation of sidewalk bulbing and stop signs at the intersection of 7fr and Magnolia.
The general rule is that an official's real property is considered to be directly
involved in a decision if it meets any of the criteria in regulation I8704.2(a) (enclosed).
This regulation states that real property (in which the official has an interesi) is directly
involved in a governmental decision when it is "located within 500 feet of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental
decision. " (Regulation I 87 0a.2@)(t).)
Therefore, Councilmember Wahl's spouse's property which is within 500 feet of
the Enloe Project - an areawhich is the subject of a govemmental decision - would be
deemed directly involved, unless an exception applies. (Brodslq Advice Letter,No. A-
03-2se.)
5. Applicable Materiatity Standard
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefitmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)
Regulation 18105.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property which is directly involved in the goverrmental decision is
presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a ny financial effect on
the real property. Therefore, Councilmember Wahl may not participate in the decisions
with respect to the Enloe Project if these decisions will have any financial effect at all -
even a penny -- on his spouse's real property interest.
6. Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely.,, (Regulation
18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices
to meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.)


File No. A-05-203
Page No. 6
It is assumed that any financial effect of a governmental decision on real property
that is directly involved in the governmental decision is material. Therefore,
Councilmember Wahl may not participatein decisions involving the Enloe project,
unless he can rebut this presumption or unless the public g..r"rrlly exception applies.
Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
advice. (In re Oglesby, (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination of
whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiahty standard will be
met, is necessarily a factual question that is ultimateiy for the councilmember to decide.
Segmentation of Decisions
Generally, decisions are analyzed independently to determine if there will be a
foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. (In re Owen
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) However, circumstances can arise where the decisions on
separate projects ar9 too inextricably related to be considered separately and, in such
cases, a public dfficial's conflict of interest in one decision will disquaiify him or her
from participating in the other decisions. If decisions are not inextri-cably ti*"a, a public
official will not have to be disqualified if those decisions can be properly segmented.
(See regulation 1 8709(a), enclosed.)
Your facts indicate that all the applications will be discussed by the city council as
one item, because all of the applications relate to an integrated projectl However,
segmentation may apply if the decisions can be broken down inlo separate decisions that
are not inextricably interrelated to the decisions in which Councilmember Wahl has a
disqualiffing conflict of interest. For instance, if decisions regarding the construction of
the parking structure and the installation of sidewalk bulbing and stop signs can be
segmented from other Enloe Project decisions, then the council *"rrt".tay be able to
participate in those other decisions. However, you have not provided specific
information about the particular projects, how they are related, or if they can be
segmented. Therefore, we are unable to further analyze this issue.
7 & 8. The Public Generally and Legaly Required Participation Exceptions
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official,s participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation iSZOS.)


File No. A-05-203
Page No. 7
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
the situation you describe, therefoie we do not addless them here.
,rr_rul[lou 
have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures

CONCLUSION: Yes. It is presumed that the financial effect of the governmental decision on

presumption is rebutted or an exception applies, he is disqualified from participating in
decisions regarding the Enloe Project if it is reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental
